# Dugong Field Notes

> A working journal from the team building Dugong, AI-native workflow automation. Essays, post-mortems, and field research on the playbooks that quietly run modern ecommerce and operations teams. Dugong lets you describe a workflow in plain language and compiles it into a running automation.

Site: https://blog.dugong.live
Publisher: Dugong (https://dugong.live)
Topics: workflow automation, AI agents, Shopify automation, ecommerce operations, support-ticket deflection, no-code/natural-language automation.

## Articles

- [The death of the drag-and-drop builder](https://blog.dugong.live/): The site's lead essay (Dispatch Nº 47). For fifteen years workflow automation looked like a canvas of rectangles connected by lines; after watching three million runs across twelve thousand teams, the Dugong team argues the canvas itself was always the problem. Makes the case for describing a workflow in plain language and letting a compiler turn it into running automation with triggers, branching, and integrations — instead of hand-wiring brittle node graphs. By Aanya Rao, 2026-05-28.
- [The tracking number you paste in by hand](https://blog.dugong.live/the-tracking-number-you-paste-by-hand.html): Entering tracking numbers is the quiet Shopify chore that scales linearly with how well you are selling, and the platform leaves it almost entirely to a human. Most physical orders do not fulfill themselves: for a self-shipped, supplier-shipped, or dropshipped order you open the order, hit mark as fulfilled, click into the tracking field, paste the number your supplier or warehouse emailed you, pick the carrier from a dropdown, save, and send the confirmation, one order at a time. Three concrete walls: auto-fulfillment only covers narrow cases (an entirely digital order, or items a connected fulfillment service or app controls and pushes its own tracking back for), so the everyday physical order defaults to manual; bulk fulfill and the CSV tracking import still leave the matching to you (bulk fulfill marks orders shipped but you attach each tracking number, and the CSV has to be assembled by hand with the right order numbers paired to the right tracking numbers and carriers, which is the exact job you were trying to avoid); and Shopify Flow, free on every plan, can react to an order, tag it, and notify you, but has no action that marks an order fulfilled with a specific tracking number and carrier read from a supplier email or feed, so the one step that clears the order is the step it cannot take, and an order assigned to a third-party fulfillment service location will not even show you the mark as fulfilled button (the service is meant to control the status, so when it is slow or silent you have to move items between locations to fulfill it yourself). Why it matters: the manual step is the most error-prone touch in the whole post-purchase flow, sitting right where the customer is paying closest attention, so a number pasted onto the wrong order, a dropped digit, a carrier dropdown left on the default, or a split order marked fully shipped all get mailed straight to the customer as a dead tracking link or a delivery clock that started before the box exists, which then comes back as a where-is-my-order ticket. The big mistake is treating it as data entry when it is reading and matching: a supplier message that was never formatted for a machine, a shipment that covers only some line items, a number reused from yesterday that should not re-fulfill anything, a tracking number that arrived for an item not even on the order. The usual workarounds break: bulk fulfill only handles the marked-as-shipped half and is blind to split orders; building a tracking CSV every morning turns a merchant into a data-entry clerk reconciling supplier formats that always diverge; and a tracking importer needs a clean mapped file or a consistent feed and cannot read a free-form email or reconcile mismatches, so the hard part stays with you. The AI playbook catches the tracking wherever it lands (email, spreadsheet, CSV, or feed), matches each number to the right order and the right line items even on split shipments, detects the carrier from the number so the link works, fulfills the matched line items at the correct location with the tracking attached, sends the shipping confirmation once per shipment (a partial when only part has gone), holds anything that does not match, repeats, or is blank for a human, and logs which number went to which order and why. By Priya Singh, 2026-06-27.
- [The deleted product that 404s your customers](https://blog.dugong.live/the-deleted-product-that-404s-your-customers.html): Redirecting deleted products is the quiet Shopify SEO leak the platform leaves entirely to the merchant. Every catalog sheds products (a discontinued line, a seasonal item, a duplicate listing cleaned up), and when you delete one Shopify does not create a redirect for its URL: the old address simply returns a 404 page not found, even though Google still has it indexed, your own internal links and external backlinks still point at it, the Google Shopping feed still lists it, and a paid ad you forgot to pause may still send traffic to it. The same silent break happens when you change a product handle, which rewrites the URL and orphans the old one, and when you unpublish rather than delete. Three concrete walls: deleting a product creates no redirect (no prompt at delete time, no default forward, nothing); the native fix is manual, one URL at a time, under Online Store then Navigation then URL Redirects, with a CSV bulk import that still requires you to know every broken URL and decide every destination first; and Shopify Flow, free on every plan, can react to a delete and send an internal note but has no action that creates a redirect, so the one step that fixes the 404 is the step it cannot take. Why it matters: the deleted page had earned link equity (a search position, backlinks, a place in internal linking), and when its URL 404s that value drains instead of passing to a live page, while the customer who clicked a real link from search, an old email, or a paid ad lands on an error and bounces, often on traffic the merchant paid for. The big mistake is redirecting every dead URL to the homepage in one blanket rule, which search engines treat as a soft 404 because the destination does not match the request, so the link equity is lost anyway and the customer is dropped somewhere they then have to search from scratch. The usual workarounds break: a blanket homepage redirect is the soft-404 trap; setting up each redirect by hand survives until the first busy week and never fires for the silent breaks (handle changes, unpublishes); and a redirect app mostly logs the 404 after a visitor already hit it and still leaves the merchant to choose which live page each broken URL should point at, so detection is solved and the judgement is not. The AI playbook catches every URL a delete, unpublish, or handle change just broke before it goes cold, reads what the page was (product type, collections, tags, title), finds the closest live page (a replacement product, the same collection, or the nearest alternative) rather than a blanket homepage redirect, writes a 301 redirect to it, rechecks later that the target is still live and in stock and repoints it if not so it never chains into a fresh dead end, holds the high-value URLs with real traffic, rankings, or backlinks for a human to confirm the destination, and logs every broken URL, chosen target, and reason so the redirect map stays auditable. By Priya Singh, 2026-06-25.
- [The unpaid order that never cancels itself](https://blog.dugong.live/the-unpaid-order-that-never-cancels-itself.html): Clearing unpaid orders is the quiet Shopify chore that holds inventory hostage, and the platform gives merchants no native way to do it. Every store that takes a manual payment method (bank transfer, cash on delivery, an unpaid invoice, or a draft order sent to a wholesale buyer) accumulates orders that were placed but never paid for: each looks like a sale in the dashboard, each commits stock, and none clears itself. When an order is placed Shopify commits the inventory to it and holds that commitment while the order sits in pending payment, so a unit nobody paid for is off the shelf and unavailable to the next customer with a card out, which is how a low-stock product or a launch can show sold out while real, paying demand bounces off it. Three concrete walls: there is no auto-cancel for unpaid orders (no native setting cancels an order still unpaid after N days; cancelling is a manual action you take one order at a time, choosing restock and notify on each, with no timer, batch, or rule); Shopify Flow, free on every plan, can react to an order and tag it but has no action that cancels an order or restocks its held inventory, so the one step that matters is the step it cannot take; and the timing and judgement are on the merchant, who must decide how long to wait for a bank transfer versus a cash-on-delivery order, whether to chase the customer first, which orders are too valuable to cancel on a timer, and what to say when they do. Why it matters: the damage is not the pending order but the inventory frozen underneath it, plus an orders list padded with sales that will never settle, which makes revenue, bestseller, and fulfillment numbers all slightly wrong and leaves the work of telling a real order from a dead one to a human, by eye, every day. The big mistake is a flat global timer, because a bank transfer, a cash-on-delivery order, and an agreed wholesale invoice settle on completely different clocks, so a rule that cancels everything at forty-eight hours kills the transfer that was always going to land on day three. The usual workarounds break: clearing them by hand every Monday stops scaling the moment volume climbs and is the first chore dropped on a busy week; a Flow rule automates the noticing and none of the doing; and a flat auto-cancel app runs a single blunt timer that treats a first-time bank transfer like a repeat wholesale buyer on net terms, fires a cold cancellation with no attempt to collect first, and cannot tell the order that just paid sixty seconds ago from the one that is truly dead. The AI playbook reads each pending order (payment method, value, and whether the customer is new, repeat, or a known wholesale account), sets the window to fit how that method settles, nudges the customer to pay once or twice with the amount and a clear way to complete it, rechecks at the deadline that it is still unpaid so a late payer is never cancelled by a stale timer, cancels the cold ones and restocks the held inventory in the same step with the reason logged as unpaid, sends a kind note that the order lapsed with a link to reorder, and holds high-value and wholesale orders for a human while logging every nudge, cancel, and restock. By Priya Singh, 2026-06-24.
- [The review request you never send](https://blog.dugong.live/the-review-request-you-never-send.html): Collecting reviews from happy customers is the highest-leverage Shopify chore most stores never get to, because the platform gives them no native way to do it. Shopify retired its own product reviews app and never replaced it, so an out-of-the-box store has no place to gather a product review and nothing that emails a customer to ask for one. Three concrete walls: there is no native review collection (the Shop channel can collect a store review, which is not a review of the item someone bought, and the request email, the on-page star widget, and the photo upload all live in a third-party app you choose, install, and wire in); Shopify Flow can start a workflow when an order is marked delivered, the correct trigger, but it does not write a personalized message, does not know skincare needs a longer wait than snacks, does not check whether the customer just opened a return, and has nowhere of its own to store the rating that comes back, so the real work still lives in the app and the app still has to be told exactly what to do; and even with an app installed the timing and judgement are on the merchant, who decides how long after delivery to wait, whether to ask again, which products are too sensitive to chase, and what happens when the reply is one star. Why it matters: a product page stacked with recent, specific, photographed reviews converts the shopper on the fence while a blank one asks them to go first on faith, and sending nothing yields the low-single-digit fraction of reviews that arrive on their own (skewed toward the angry), whereas a timed request after delivery lifts response into double digits from the satisfied majority who would never have thought to write one. The single biggest lever on response rate is tying the request to delivery, not fulfilment, and tuning the delay to the product: a few days for consumables, about a week for apparel, longer for anything someone has to live with before forming a view. The usual workarounds break: emailing customers yourself is fine for the first week and stops by the second busy one; a Flow rule on delivered needs a review app bolted on and even then fires the same blunt action for everyone, happily asking the customer with an open return to please rate their purchase; and a review app on default settings sends a generic how-did-we-do to everyone on the same timer, better than silence and well short of a request that reads the order. The AI playbook waits for the right window after delivery tuned to the product, reads the order before asking (who bought what, repeat or first-time, and whether there is an open return, refund, or support thread) and skips anyone mid-complaint, writes a personalized ask that names the real items instead of "your recent order", sends once and follows up a single time if there is no reply then stops, routes a high rating to the public review and the storefront and a low rating privately to support before it lands in public, remembers who was asked and when so no one is asked twice and a multi-item order is one ask, and logs every send, response, and rating while flagging the edge cases a human should eyeball. By Priya Singh, 2026-06-22.
- [The repeat customer your store treats like a stranger](https://blog.dugong.live/the-repeat-customer-your-store-treats-like-a-stranger.html): Customer segmentation is the growth lever most Shopify stores never actually pull, and the native tooling stops well short of it. After a couple of years of trading the customers page is a flat list of names, an order count, and an amount spent, with nothing that marks a loyal regular worth protecting, a lapsing customer worth winning back, or a one-time discount hunter not worth another code. Three concrete walls: tagging is manual by default (you can add or remove a customer tag by hand, one profile at a time or in a bulk action, but nothing watches behavior and applies it for you); the one automated path is Shopify Flow, free on every plan, which can add a tag when a single blunt thing becomes true, such as total spend over a number, and cannot weigh recency against frequency against value or notice a high spender whose orders have stopped; and the segments feature is a filter you read, not a durable label that travels with the customer and updates as their behavior changes, so it cannot be keyed off by the rest of your stack. Why it matters: selling again to someone who already trusts you is far cheaper than acquiring a stranger, and a small core of repeat buyers usually drives a large share of revenue, but you can only act on that if you can tell the groups apart at the moment you send. When the list is flat, your VIPs get nothing for being VIPs, your lapsing regulars drift away in silence (the single most recoverable customer you have, invisible until they are long gone), your discount hunters get more discounts and the whole base learns to wait for a code, and support treats a first-time buyer and an eleven-order regular with the same urgency. The standard read is recency, frequency, and monetary value together: two customers who both spent six hundred dollars can be a two-year loyal regular and a one-time deep-discount buyer, and a spend threshold tags them identically. The usual workarounds break: hand-tagging captures a snapshot that rots (a customer tagged vip in March still wears it in December, now misleading every flow that reads it), a Flow rule on spend cannot reason across several signals at once and rarely removes tags cleanly, and a segmentation or loyalty app mostly runs the same rigid thresholds in a nicer interface while the truth about your customers now lives in a second system that has to be kept in sync. The AI playbook pulls each customer's full history (orders, total and recent spend, recency, frequency, products, returns, discount use, support contacts), reads the pattern instead of a single threshold, assigns one clear lifecycle tag from a clean vocabulary and removes the ones that no longer fit, re-evaluates as new orders, returns, and cancellations land so a VIP who goes quiet becomes at-risk instead of a stale VIP forever, writes the tags back through the Admin API without touching anything else on the profile, hands the segment to the systems that act on it (email, discounts, support priority, ad audiences), and logs every change with its reason while flagging the edge cases a human should eyeball. By Priya Singh, 2026-06-21.
- [The image alt text no one writes](https://blog.dugong.live/the-image-alt-text-no-one-writes.html): Image alt text is the Shopify accessibility-and-SEO chore everyone assumes is handled and is not. Alt text is the short written description attached to an image, and it does two jobs: a screen reader reads it aloud to a shopper who cannot see the photo, and a search engine uses it to understand what the image shows (which is how product photos surface in image search). Shopify stores an alt text on every image but never writes one, has no bulk editor and no audit view to show which images are blank, and does not generate a description from the image (the file name is not alt text), so the field starts empty and stays empty until a human opens each product, clicks into the media, and types a line. A store with a few hundred products at several photos each is thousands of empty alt fields with no native path to fill them. Three concrete walls: there is no bulk edit and no way to even see which images are missing alt text; nothing is filled in by default and the file name (IMG_4821.jpg) is not alt text; and the one bulk path, the product CSV's Image Alt Text column, only moves the data (you still write every line), covers only product images and not theme or content images, and risks a re-import overwriting neighboring fields. Why it matters: a blank alt means a screen reader user hears nothing or hears the file name instead of the product (missing alt text is the single most common accessibility failure cited in the demand letters now landing on ecommerce stores, under standards whose enforcement keeps rising), and it means a search engine and the newer AI answer engines have nothing to read, so the image is effectively invisible to them. Good alt text is short (roughly under 125 characters, where many screen readers cut off), describes what is actually in the picture in plain language rather than stuffing keywords (which reads badly aloud and looks like spam), and leaves genuinely decorative images with an empty alt so readers skip them. The usual workarounds all break: writing them by hand is right but never finishes past a small catalog and is the first thing dropped on a launch week; the CSV moves data but still leaves you describing every image and misses every non-product image; and a cheap bulk app templates the line from the product title plus variant, so the front, the back, and the detail shot all read the same and none describes the actual photo. The AI playbook finds every product, variant, collection, and content image whose alt is missing, duplicated, or just the file name, looks at the image itself plus the product context (title, type, variant, color, material), writes a concise accurate line under about 125 characters in the store's voice, keeps near-identical variant and angle shots distinct, leaves decorative images with an empty alt, writes the alt text back through the Admin API without touching anything else, re-checks new uploads so it never drifts back to blank, and logs every line while flagging the images too generic or low-quality to describe well. By Priya Singh, 2026-06-20.
- [The product descriptions you'll never finish writing](https://blog.dugong.live/the-product-descriptions-youll-never-finish-writing.html): Writing product copy at catalog scale is the Shopify job that looks solved and is half-solved. Shopify Magic is the AI assistant built into the admin and will draft a product description from a few keywords you type, with a tone setting, which genuinely helps for one product. Four concrete walls show up the moment you think about the whole catalog: it works one product at a time inside the editor and there is no native bulk button (the known workaround is to export products to a CSV, add a prompt column, run it through an AI tool, and re-import, which is brittle and breaks in small ways on a large file); it writes from the keywords you hand it, not from the product's variants and options, supplier spec sheet, materials list, or images, so the context that makes copy specific and true still gets typed in by hand for every SKU; the draft reads as bland and recognizably machine-made with no brand voice, so you rewrite every one and the work just moves from writing to editing without the catalog getting any shorter; and nothing stops the catalog repeating itself, so ten near-identical variants come out as ten near-identical descriptions. Why it matters: on a product page with no clerk the description is the only salesperson, so a blank one loses the sale to a competitor page that answers the buyer's question, and it is also the page's whole case to a search engine. A blank description gives Google nothing to rank; a line pasted from the supplier is already live word-for-word on every other store running the same feed, so yours has no reason to surface; and bulk-generated near-twins compete with each other as duplicate content instead of each earning its own ranking. The usual workarounds all break: writing them by hand is the best quality but never finishes and is the first thing dropped on a launch week; using the built-in AI per product relocates the chore to editing without applying any consistent voice; and a one-click bulk app delivers speed but, fed thin inputs, produces a large volume of generic, near-duplicate copy you now have to audit and re-edit. The AI playbook reads each product whole (title, type and tags, every variant and option, the existing copy, the supplier spec sheet, and the product images), writes the description in one defined brand voice applied the same way across every product from a short brief instead of per-product tone toggles, differentiates near-identical products so each page earns its own ranking and never pastes the supplier's shared copy, structures each description for SEO and on-page scanning (a lead line, the specifics a buyer needs, one focus phrase, no keyword stuffing), writes the descriptions back across the whole catalog through the Admin API rather than one editor box at a time, backfills the products that ship blank or with placeholder copy and refreshes when the product, price, or spec changes, and flags the products too thin to describe well while logging every description with the inputs it was written from. By Priya Singh, 2026-06-19.
- [The order tag you set by hand](https://blog.dugong.live/the-order-tag-you-set-by-hand.html): Order tagging is the Shopify job that looks fully solved from a distance and is half-solved up close. Shopify Flow is free on every plan and tags an order well when one condition is true (a total over a threshold, a specific product in the cart), but the tags merchants actually set by hand are compound: a high-value order, going to a slow-ship destination, from a repeat customer, with a free-text gift note, all at once. Three concrete walls: Flow checks one condition at a time, so a dozen multi-signal tags become a dozen overlapping workflows that are hard to reason about and easy to break; it can reference structured note attributes and line item custom attributes but cannot read a free-text order note the way a person does ("please ship before Friday", "no invoice, it is a gift"), which is the exact signal someone scans the orders list for every morning; and the tags drift and break, because nothing keeps the vocabulary clean (vip, VIP, and Vip coexist and a segment built on one silently misses the others) and a tag built from a variable can hit invalid characters or the length limit and throw an order-tags-is-invalid error that fails silently. Why it matters: the tag is the connective tissue between an order and everything downstream, so fulfillment routing reads it to pick a location or 3PL, other automations trigger off it, customer and order segments are built on it, exports and reports group by it, and email platforms read it to choose a sequence; a wrong or missing tag misroutes a shipment, never fires a flow, drops a customer from a sequence, or undercounts a segment. There is also a related Admin API limit: you cannot change line item properties on an order that already exists, only order-level tags and notes. The usual workarounds all break: hand-tagging every morning stops scaling exactly when volume climbs (a sale or launch) and is the first thing dropped when the day gets busy; a Flow per tag automates the typing but inherits a maintenance pile and still cannot read the note; a tagging app buys more conditions, not a reader, so the free-text and judgement calls stay outside what a rule can see. The AI playbook reads the whole order at once (line items and properties, the note and note attributes, the discount and source, the destination, and the customer's history), decides the tags from plain-language rules that combine product, value, customer, destination, and free-text note signals instead of one condition at a time, normalizes every tag against one controlled vocabulary so there are no invalid characters, near-duplicate casing, or over-length strings, writes the tags back at the order level without touching anything else, triggers what the tag is for (route to a location, place a hold, set a priority, hand off to the 3PL), backfills the orders that predate the rule so segments and reports are not half-blind, and logs every tag with its reason while flagging the orders too ambiguous to tag with confidence. By Priya Singh, 2026-06-18.
- [The meta description you write one at a time](https://blog.dugong.live/the-meta-description-you-write-one-at-a-time.html): The unique meta description is the Shopify SEO chore everyone assumes is a setting and is not. Shopify stores a meta description field for every product, collection, and page, but the only bulk option in the admin writes the same text onto every selected product, which is precisely the duplicate, boilerplate text search guidance tells you to avoid, so the fast path produces a worse result than doing nothing. A genuinely unique description has to be written in each item's search engine listing one product at a time, and the community asks for a real bulk fix in threads titled, almost verbatim, "can you fix meta descriptions in bulk so they don't all sound the same." Three concrete walls: bulk editing only sets them all identical; leaving the field blank does not mean nothing but means generic, because Shopify auto-generates the search snippet from the page content (usually the opening of the product description, often cut off mid-sentence and nearly identical across similar products); and every surface (products, collections, homepage, standard pages, every blog post) needs its own, edited individually, with the high-intent collection pages most likely to have none. Why it matters: the meta description does not move ranking directly and Google may rewrite it per query, but it decides the click-through between two listings in the same position, on free traffic you already rank for, and answer engines and AI search lean on a concise, distinct line to decide what a page is about and what to quote, so a blank or duplicated field gives them nothing to distinguish. Length guidance is ~150 to 160 characters (desktop truncates past that, mobile shows less, no penalty for going long), but uniqueness and matching search intent matter more than the exact count. The usual workarounds all break: writing them all by hand is right but never finished past a small catalog, so the long tail that lives on search goes uncovered; a CSV/spreadsheet export moves the data but still leaves you authoring every line and risks a re-import overwriting neighboring fields; and a template SEO app generates lines that are technically unique (the product name differs) and functionally identical (every one reads from the same mould), the duplicate-content problem in a costume, plus a monthly bill. The AI playbook finds every product, collection, and page whose meta is missing, duplicated, too long, or too short, reads each page (title, description, key attributes, price band, buyer intent) to write a distinct line in the store's voice that leads with the intent and fits the ~155-character window, checks it against every other description so no two are identical or templated, fixes the weak meta title in the same pass, writes the field back through the API in batches without disturbing price or inventory, logs every change for a human spot-check, and re-checks new products as they are created so the field never silently drifts back to blank. By Priya Singh, 2026-06-17.
- [The sale you have to start by hand](https://blog.dugong.live/the-sale-you-start-by-hand.html): The timed sale is the Shopify chore everyone assumes is a setting and is not. Shopify lets you change a product's price and set a compare-at price for the struck-through was-price, but it has no native way to schedule that price change to start and end on its own: the edit applies the instant you save, and Shopify Flow cannot schedule a price change (the community answer to that exact request has been no). So a storewide or by-collection sale falls to a human who flips the prices by hand at the moment the sale should begin (often at midnight in the customer's timezone, where a single timezone slip starts or ends it in the wrong place), babysits it, then has to put every original price back when it ends, because Shopify does not remember the pre-sale price for you. Three concrete gaps: no scheduled start or end on a price, no automatic revert (the forgotten reset leaves bestsellers on sale for days, a margin leak with no campaign attached), and no targeting by rule (no native way to say "everything in summer, except the styles already on clearance, never below a margin floor" and apply it in one move). The usual workarounds all break: changing prices by hand makes you the cron job at both edges; importing a sale CSV at the start and a revert CSV at the end is bulk but still applies immediately, not on a schedule, and a wrong revert file pushes stale prices over live ones; a discount code is scheduleable but shows no struck-through price while customers browse (the storefront reads full price until checkout) and invites forgotten codes, coupon-site leaks, and unintended stacking; and a sale-scheduler app ships with its author's idea of how a sale works, a monthly bill that climbs with SKU count, and your real rules squeezed into someone else's toggles. The stakes are real: flash sales lift transaction rates by ~35% on average and convert ~48% better on mobile, but overusing markdowns trains customers to wait, so the value is in precision (the exact products for the exact window) that doing it by hand cannot keep. The AI playbook selects the products in scope by rule (collection, tag, or vendor, minus anything already on clearance), records each product's current price so the revert is exact, applies the discount and sets the compare-at to the old price so the strikethrough shows while never pricing below the margin floor, skips and flags anything that would fall below cost or is already discounted deeper, confirms the sale is live on a sample product, reverts every price to the recorded original at the end time with nothing left behind, and logs the run so the next sale is a one-line repeat. By Priya Singh, 2026-06-16.
- [The reorder you always make too late](https://blog.dugong.live/the-reorder-you-always-make-too-late.html): Supplier replenishment is the quiet, expensive Shopify gap that no native feature closes and merchants on the community forums keep asking for. Shopify tracks inventory and offers a purchase order screen, but it has no engine that reads your sales and stock, decides what to reorder and how much, and drafts the PO; merchants who find the PO feature widely call it "too basic." Three concrete walls: there is no per-SKU reorder point (let alone one derived from how fast an item sells and how long its supplier takes), the purchase order does not auto-populate the product cost you already entered and has no native field for the supplier's own SKU (so you retype prices and cross-reference part numbers by hand), and there is no real reporting on what is on the way, partially received, or still pending, so nothing connects the on-hand count to the lead-time calendar. The cost is large and steady: stockouts cost retailers ~$1.2 trillion a year in lost sales worldwide, about 8% of SKUs are out of stock at any moment, roughly half of all products hit at least one stockout a year, the average stockout lasts ~35 days, and about 43% of shoppers buy from a competitor when an item is unavailable. The mirror cost is overordering: buy too much and cash is frozen in slow movers, plus storage and markdown risk, so reordering too late and reordering too much are the same planning failure in opposite directions. Covers why a reorder spreadsheet (a stale snapshot that misses the fast and seasonal SKUs that matter most), a single flat reorder point for the whole catalog (too late for a fast mover with a long lead time, too early for a slow one), and a Shopify Flow low-stock alert (a notification, not a plan) all fail, and how an AI playbook projects days of cover per SKU from recent pace, trend, and seasonality, subtracts each supplier's real lead time to find the must-reorder-by date, right-sizes the buy against both stockouts and idle cash, groups items by supplier to clear order minimums, drafts the PO with the right cost, supplier SKU, and quantities, flags spikes and imminent stockouts, and hands the drafted orders to the merchant to approve before anything sends. By Priya Singh, 2026-06-15.
- [The abandoned cart that gets one email](https://blog.dugong.live/the-abandoned-cart-that-gets-one-email.html): Abandoned cart recovery is the highest-volume, most-complained-about Shopify revenue leak, and the native fix is thin. Shopify's built-in abandoned checkout recovery sends a single email on one of a few preset timers, with no follow-up sequence and no branching logic; to run a real cadence (for example a nudge at one hour, a reminder the next day, an incentive on the third) you have to move to a marketing platform like Klaviyo or Omnisend or stitch a Shopify Flow workaround, and even then the copy is usually identical for everyone. The one native email is also generic: it cannot tell a first-time visitor who balked at shipping from a returning customer who got interrupted, so it cannot match the message to the reason. Worst of all, since Shopify moved most stores to one-page checkout the customer's email and phone are often only saved when the whole page is completed (the old three-page checkout saved them after the first step), so many abandoned checkouts arrive with no contact at all and no one to send to, and merchants below Shopify Plus cannot toggle back to three-page checkout. The leak is large: the average cart abandonment rate is about 70% (roughly seven in ten filled carts never convert on their own), a single generic reminder recovers only about 3 to 8 percent, and a timed personalized sequence across email and SMS recovers meaningfully more, all of it the cheapest revenue a store touches because the demand was already paid for. Covers why relying on the native email, building a Flow ladder, and bolting on a full marketing platform all fall short (recovery is a judgement-and-timing problem, not a scheduling one), and how an AI playbook catches every recoverable cart, recovers the contact where it can by matching to a known customer, reads the cart and shopper to guess the objection, sends a first nudge fast on the channel they prefer with copy that fits the reason and no discount yet, follows up once more addressing the likely objection, offers a measured incentive only when it makes sense, stops the instant they purchase, suppresses anyone already in another flow, escalates high-value or VIP carts to a human, and alerts the merchant if recovery suddenly drops (a sign checkout is eating contacts). By Priya Singh, 2026-06-14.
- [The sold-out products that sink your collection](https://blog.dugong.live/the-sold-out-products-that-sink-your-collection.html): Out-of-stock merchandising is a long-running, heavily-upvoted Shopify forum complaint everyone assumes is a setting and is not. Shopify has no reliable way to push sold-out products to the bottom of a collection page: some sort options and theme code push out-of-stock down within a single page, but once pagination kicks in each page sorts itself, so a later page can be all sold-out while the first is mixed, and Shopify's Liquid sort filter has no inventory_quantity parameter so theme code cannot sort cleanly across the whole collection either. The only native, reliable option is dragging products by hand in a manual sort, which does not scale past a few small collections. Hiding with a smart-collection "inventory greater than 0" condition is all-or-nothing: it only works on automated collections (not manual ones), does not apply to site search, and removes the product entirely, throwing away its SEO ranking and the back-in-stock signup on the page. The cost is large: stockouts account for roughly 40% of lost sales, nearly 7 in 10 shoppers leave without buying when they cannot find what they came for (often for a competitor), about 51% of products hit at least one stockout a year and stay unavailable ~35 days on average, and the typical out-of-stock rate sits near 8% (climbing to ~10% on promotional items), so at any moment about one tile in twelve is a dead end and it is worse on a sale day. A sold-out product at the top of a collection does not just lose its own sale, it drags down the conversion of every in-stock product behind it. Covers why hand-reordering, smart-collection hiding, and theme code or apps all fail (the real problem is merchandising judgement and freshness, not a one-time sort), and how an AI playbook watches a real inventory change across every collection and search surface, demotes a freshly sold-out product below the in-stock items instead of off the page (keeping its URL and notify-me form alive), keeps items restocking within the week visible with a back-soon badge, protects hero and high-ranking products from being buried, applies per-surface rules (demote on browse, drop from search, leave hand-curated edits to the merchant's rules), restores original positions automatically on restock, and flags a collection that has gone mostly empty. By Priya Singh, 2026-06-13.
- [The restock alert that never fires](https://blog.dugong.live/the-restock-alert-that-never-fires.html): Back-in-stock notification is the Shopify gap everyone assumes is a setting and is not. Shopify has no built-in feature that emails or texts customers when an out-of-stock product is restocked; some themes show a "notify me" form, but it only collects an email into a list and does not watch inventory or send anything, so the standard advice in the forums is to export the addresses and message people by hand. The cost is large and recoverable: stockouts account for roughly 40% of lost sales, the lost sales ratio (revenue missed because items were unavailable) runs about 20 to 30% in ecommerce, about 69% of online shoppers buy from a competitor rather than wait, and a stocked-out product stays unavailable for an average of around 35 days. A back-in-stock request is the warmest possible lead because the shopper has named exactly what they want and given you a way to reach them, yet in most stores the request lands in a void. Covers the three failure modes (the request goes nowhere, a single mass blast sends the whole waitlist at once so the units sell to whoever clicks fastest while everyone else hits a sold-out page, and an alert fired off an unsynced count causes overselling), and why manual emailing, one big announcement, and Shopify Flow or a basic app all fail (the judgement is who to tell, in what order, throttled to real units, on which channel, and when to stop). The AI playbook watches a real (synced) inventory change, pulls the waitlist for that exact variant by wait time, notifies first-requested-first only as many people as there are units, sends SMS or email per the channel the customer chose with a deep link to the variant, throttles the next wave only if more stock lands, closes the list on sellthrough, and escalates hyped drops or tiny restocks to a human before anything sends. By Priya Singh, 2026-06-13.
- [The chargeback you lose by default](https://blog.dugong.live/the-chargeback-you-lose-by-default.html): Chargebacks are the Shopify pain where merchants do the work and still lose, because doing nothing looks almost identical to doing something. With Shopify Payments, Shopify auto-populates the basic transaction data and submits a response to the issuing bank on the deadline date, but it does not read the reason code, match the evidence to it, or write a rebuttal, so the default response is a generic packet filed on the last day. Merchants get a short one-shot window (usually 7–21 days) to add the evidence that actually wins, and once the response is sent or the deadline passes nothing more can be submitted and the bank's decision is final (no appeal). Win rates are low: US merchants win ~54% of disputes they fight through representment, and only ~17% of fraud-coded chargebacks. Friendly fraud (a real customer disputing a real, delivered purchase) is now ~75% of chargebacks and rising at a double-digit annual rate. The all-in cost of a single dispute is ~$315 (avg transaction ~$84) once the fee, lost goods, shipping, and staff time are counted; ecommerce chargebacks are projected at ~$33.8B in 2025. Winning requires reason-code-specific evidence scattered across systems (AVS/CVV match + device/IP for fraud, carrier tracking + signature for item-not-received, listing + accepted refund policy for not-as-described). Covers why leaning on Shopify's auto-response, pre-emptively refunding risky orders, and Shopify Flow all fail (a chargeback is a reason code attached to a story, not a clean trigger), and how an AI playbook reads the reason code, decides if a dispute is winnable or worth conceding, gathers the matching evidence from the processor/carrier/help desk, writes a point-by-point rebuttal, files it well before the deadline, and escalates high-value, ambiguous, or repeat-disputer cases to a human. By Priya Singh, 2026-06-11.
- [The return that won't approve itself](https://blog.dugong.live/the-return-that-wont-approve-itself.html): Returns are the most-complained-about Shopify workflow that scales directly with sales, and Shopify's native handling leaves the hard parts manual. Self-serve returns let a customer request a return but every request still has to be manually approved or declined in the admin (Shopify Flow cannot fully automate approval); true exchanges are not supported (customers can't request one and there are no exchange-specific rules, so a size/colour swap means refunding or cancelling and rebuilding the order by hand, or bolting on an app); return policies can only be set by delivery date and product, not order tags, customer type, or fulfilment date; and refunds go only to the original payment method, so store credit is another manual workaround. Because the refund is the path of least resistance and ~45% of returns are sizing/fit/colour, sales that could have stayed sales walk out as money returned. ~19–20% of online orders are returned (NRF 2025; apparel 20–40%). Returns automation sharply cuts return-related support tickets and speeds processing, and a smooth return experience makes shoppers far more likely to buy again. Covers why "process them faster", manual store-credit nudges, and Shopify Flow all fail (a return request is a natural-language judgement call, not a structured trigger), and how an AI playbook matches the message to the order, checks eligibility, offers an exchange first when stock allows, creates the replacement order and label, settles the price delta, issues credit or refund, confirms in plain language, and escalates only the risky cases (out of window, high-value, repeat returner, damaged item). By Priya Singh, 2026-06-09.
- [The order change that races your warehouse](https://blog.dugong.live/the-order-change-that-races-your-warehouse.html): The post-purchase order-change request ("can you change my order?") is one of the most common and most-complained-about Shopify support tickets. After checkout Shopify gives the customer no self-serve way to fix a typo, swap a size/variant, change quantity, add an item, or update the shipping address; every change routes through the merchant and only while the order is unfulfilled — the moment you click Fulfil items most edits lock, and the billing address can never be edited. So the request becomes a footrace between the customer's email and your own warehouse. ~60–80% of ecommerce tickets are automatable and order changes are among the biggest; ~69% of shoppers prefer self-serve; structured self-serve/automated editing cuts order-change tickets ~40–60%. Covers why "just reply fast", the cancel-and-recreate dance, and Shopify Flow all fail (a change request is a natural-language message, not a structured trigger), and how an AI playbook matches the message to the order, checks fulfilment status, applies safe changes before the lock, settles the price/shipping delta (payment link or refund), confirms in plain language, and escalates only risky cases. By Priya Singh, 2026-06-08.
- [The bad address that ships anyway](https://blog.dugong.live/the-bad-address-that-ships-anyway.html): Bad-address handling is the Shopify pain merchants only notice after it costs them. Shopify runs address validation on every order and shows a yellow warning (incomplete/off) or red error (likely undeliverable), but the flags are advisory — it still lets you fulfil, so during a busy week the warning scrolls past and the package ships to an address that bounces. ~5–10% of packages are returned to sender, with incorrect/incomplete addresses the leading cause; carriers make 1–3 attempts before returning, so a late catch means a return-to-sender fee, a re-ship, a WISMO ticket, and often a refund — many times the original shipping cost. Covers why "watch more carefully" and Flow tag-and-hold both fail (Flow can flag a bad address but can't fix it, and Shopify won't recalc shipping after an edit), and how an AI playbook verifies every address before fulfilment, silently applies confident corrections, emails the customer a suggested fix when ambiguous, and escalates only the unresolvable ones. By Priya Singh, 2026-06-06.
- [The high-risk flag that makes you guess](https://blog.dugong.live/the-high-risk-flag-that-makes-you-guess.html): Shopify's "high-risk" fraud flag is a blackbox that relocates a decision instead of removing it — it scores every order but hands the judgement call back to a merchant who has even less data than the algorithm. Each flagged order is a coin-flip between two expensive mistakes: fulfil and risk a chargeback (avg ~$191, ~$3.75 lost per $1 of fraud, Visa watches at a 0.65% rate), or cancel and risk a false decline (~$443B/yr lost industry-wide vs ~$48B to real fraud; loyal customers wrongly declined cut reorders 65% and 27% never return). Covers why Flow's auto-cancel and tag-and-hold both fail, and how an AI playbook enriches each flagged order with the signals a human would check, auto-decides the clear cases, and escalates only genuine grey areas with a full dossier. By Priya Singh, 2026-06-06.
- [The overselling problem no one automates](https://blog.dugong.live/the-overselling-problem-no-one-automates.html): Overselling — selling stock you no longer have — is one of the most-complained-about Shopify pain points and the hardest to recover from: a missed sale costs a customer you never had, but an oversold order breaks a promise to one you already won. It starts when multiple channels share one stock pool with no real-time sync; ~40% of retailers cancel at least 1 in 10 orders due to inaccurate inventory data. Covers why manual sync, safety buffers, and Flow rules fail during a sale, and how an AI playbook keeps every channel's count honest in real time and catches oversells before fulfilment. By Priya Singh, 2026-06-05.
- [The "where is my order?" tickets that eat your afternoon](https://blog.dugong.live/the-wismo-tickets-that-eat-your-afternoon.html): WISMO ("where is my order?") is the single most common ecommerce support ticket — ~18% of inbox volume on a normal week, over 50% during a sale. Fully-loaded cost is $5–$25 per interaction. Covers why canned auto-replies fail and how an AI playbook answers tracking questions automatically, often before the customer asks. By Priya Singh, 2026-06-04.
- [The Shopify automations no one builds](https://blog.dugong.live/the-shopify-automations-no-one-builds.html): A field study of 1,374 Shopify automation workflows. Tagging is 14% of everything merchants automate; returns and refunds — the work that actually scales with order volume — are under 1%. The gap between what's easy to automate and what's worth automating. By Priya Singh, 2026-05-28.

## About Dugong

- [Dugong product site](https://dugong.live): AI-native automation platform. Describe a playbook in natural language; Dugong's compiler turns it into a running workflow with triggers, branching logic, and integrations.

## Resources

- [RSS feed](https://blog.dugong.live/feed.xml): Full feed of new dispatches as they publish.
- [Sitemap](https://blog.dugong.live/sitemap.xml): All canonical URLs.
